summary: The post argues that while the Eric Garner case demonstrates body cameras aren't a magic solution to police brutality, they remain valuable tools for building evidence and shifting public opinion. O'Neil contends that video footage should be viewed as systematic data collection that enables comparison to baselines of 'normal police behavior.' She draws an analogy to marketing data collection, noting that understanding patterns across many incidents is more valuable than any single case. The post also highlights a fundamental power imbalance: data is typically collected on the powerless while the powerful remain opaque. O'Neil advocates for more surveillance of those in power—police, corporations, politicians—while acknowledging the challenges of ensuring public access to such footage.
key_points: A single number or video is meaningless without a baseline for comparison—we need aggregate data on police behavior across precincts, situations, and time periods
The Eric Garner video already achieved progress by galvanizing nationwide protests and shifting public opinion, even without an indictment
Data collection typically targets the powerless; we should flip this dynamic and collect more data on police, corporations, and politicians
Video evidence is vulnerable to manipulation through selective recording—officers turning cameras off at critical moments undermines the system
Body cameras should be viewed as evidence-collecting devices for public discussion, not as a panacea that will solve problems overnight
money_quotes: I am no data evangelist, but neither am I someone who dismisses data. It can be powerful and we should use its power when we can.
A number by itself is usually meaningless. What we need is to compare that number to a baseline.

In general data is being collected on the powerless, on the consumers, on the citizens, or the job applicants, and we should be pushing for more and better data to be collected instead on the powerful, on the police, on the corporations, and on the politicians.
There's a reason there is a burgeoning privacy industry for rich and powerful people.
We should think it as a mere evidence collecting device, and use it in the public discussion of how the most powerful among us treat the least powerful.
We want to see the many examples of good and great policing as well as the few examples of terrible, escalating policing.
key_insight: Body cameras should be understood not as accountability silver bullets but as systematic data collection tools that derive their power from enabling comparisons across many incidents, shifting the surveillance paradigm to focus on the powerful rather than the powerless.
